serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (approximately 700 g) is the standard commercial serving size for this beverage as listed on GrabFood Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Milk-based beverages and analogous components (fresh whole milk, brown sugar, tapioca pearls)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for Brown Sugar Deerioca Fresh Milk as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for fresh whole milk (primary component ~70% by volume), brown sugar syrup, and cooked tapioca pearls. The input per_100g values (energy 70 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 3.0 g, carbohydrate 8.0 g, sugar 7.6 g, sodium 30 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, calcium 90 mg, iron 0.07 mg, cholesterol 10 mg, saturated fat 1.8 g, trans fat 0.1 g) are consistent with a fresh-milk-dominant beverage diluted with brown sugar syrup and tapioca pearls, and are retained as the best available estimate. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) used as secondary cross-check for protein, fat, and calcium plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — tapioca and milk-based beverage analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Brown Sugar Deerioca Fresh Milk as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) cooked tapioca pearls GI ~70 (University of Sydney GI Database; tapioca, boiled in salted water, GI=70, glucose reference); (2) fresh whole milk GI ~27–34 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) brown sugar GI ~65 (analogous to sucrose/raw sugar per University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite estimate for a beverage dominated by milk (~60%) with tapioca pearls (~20%) and brown sugar syrup (~20%) yields an approximate blended GI of 65. Glycemic load per 700 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 56.0) / 100 = 36.4. Carbohydrate per 700 g serving = 8.0 g/100g × 7 = 56.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (7.6 g/100 g) from brown sugar syrup in a beverage exceeds the AHA 2018 threshold of 5 g/100 g for sugar-sweetened beverages, independently raising blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened milk beverage and choose plain unsweetened milk or water instead, as the 7.6 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of the relatively low sodium content of 30 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 7.6 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre, combined with high-GI tapioca pearls (GI ~70) and brown sugar syrup, produces rapid postprandial glucose excursions; a 700 g serving delivers 56 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 36.4.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; the combination of liquid sugar, high-GI tapioca pearls, and zero dietary fibre causes rapid blood glucose spikes inconsistent with ADA glycaemic management targets, and plain unsweetened milk is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.8 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g from fresh milk and processing, combined with 7.6 g/100 g added sugar, contribute to atherogenic lipid profiles and endothelial dysfunction risk when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this beverage due to its saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and high added sugar (7.6 g/100 g), all of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.6 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory mediator activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the high added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; plain milk providing calcium and vitamin D is a preferable alternative for bone protection.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Deerioca Fresh Milk is a popular Singaporean milk-based beverage consisting of fresh whole milk combined with brown sugar syrup and chewy tapioca pearls, commercially branded as 'Deerioca' pearls. The drink is served chilled and is assembled by layering cooked tapioca pearls coated in warm brown sugar syrup at the base of a cup, then pouring cold fresh milk over them, creating a visually striking tiger-stripe pattern as the syrup streaks through the milk. The texture is creamy and smooth from the fresh milk, punctuated by the soft, chewy bite of the tapioca pearls. The flavour profile is sweet and milky with a distinctive caramel-molasses note from the brown sugar. Nutritionally, per 100 g the beverage provides approximately 70 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 3.0 g fat (1.8 g saturated), 8.0 g carbohydrate (7.6 g sugar), 0.0 g dietary fibre, 30 mg sodium, 90 mg calcium, and 0.07 mg iron. A standard large cup serving of 700 g delivers approximately 490 kcal, 56 g carbohydrate, and a glycemic load of 36.4, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. The beverage is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension due to its high added sugar content and high glycemic load. It is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy. Originating from the Taiwanese bubble tea tradition and popularised across Singapore and Southeast Asia, it is commonly consumed as a snack or dessert beverage.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Brown Sugar Deerioca Fresh Milk as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo component entries (fresh whole milk, brown sugar, tapioca pearls) and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole milk data. The per_100g values from the input are consistent with a milk-dominant beverage and are retained as the best available estimate. GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite milk-beverage is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite beverage are not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this exact formulation as of 2024. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed as negligible/zero for a beverage composed primarily of fresh milk and brown sugar syrup; tapioca pearls contribute negligible dietary fibre per HPB and USDA data. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — fresh milk and brown sugar syrup contain no meaningful vitamin C per HPB and USDA sources. 5. Portions in the input contained duplicates and inconsistencies (two entries of 700 g both marked is_default true, two entries of 500 g both marked is_default true); the serving size of 700 g (1 large cup) has been adopted as the default serving for glycemic load calculation. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) has been corrected to the canonical HPB Foodo URL (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/). 7. GI of 65 is a composite analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this specific product has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024.
